questions.objective_ref: Distinguish a syntax error from an exception.
questions.feedback: Syntax errors are caught before running and fixed by editing code; exceptions are runtime events you plan for and handle.

questions.objective_ref: Describe how built-in exceptions are organized into a class hierarchy; catching a parent catches its children.
questions.feedback: LookupError is the parent of IndexError and KeyError, so except LookupError catches both.

questions.objective_ref: Write a try/except block to catch an exception and keep a program from crashing.
questions.feedback: int('hello') raises ValueError; execution diverts to the matching except and the program continues.

questions.objective_ref: Use else and finally clauses to separate the success path from guaranteed cleanup.
questions.feedback: else runs only on success; finally runs no matter what. Distractors are the common swaps.

questions.objective_ref: Handle several error types with multiple except blocks; first match wins.
questions.feedback: Python checks except blocks top-to-bottom and runs the first that matches, not the narrowest or the last.

questions.objective_ref: Order except blocks specific-to-general because the first match wins.
questions.feedback: A parent listed before its child shadows the child, making that block unreachable (not a syntax error).

questions.objective_ref: Use a grouped except (A, B) to give several error types one shared response.
questions.feedback: The tuple is an OR: either IndexError or ValueError triggers the shared handler.

questions.objective_ref: Recognize ValueError (right type, wrong value) vs TypeError (wrong type).
questions.feedback: int('cat') is a ValueError (right type, bad content); len(5) is a TypeError (wrong type).

questions.objective_ref: Recognize the common built-in exceptions and what triggers each (KeyError).
questions.feedback: Looking up a missing dictionary key raises KeyError, not IndexError, AttributeError, or ValueError.

questions.objective_ref: Recognize the common built-in exceptions (IOError is an alias for OSError, the FileNotFoundError family).
questions.feedback: In modern Python IOError is an alias for OSError, the same family FileNotFoundError belongs to.

questions.objective_ref: Signal an error yourself with raise.
questions.feedback: raise signals a new exception from your own code, stopping execution; a caller can catch it like a built-in.

questions.objective_ref: Define a custom exception by subclassing Exception.
questions.feedback: A custom exception is just a class inheriting from Exception; its body can be a single docstring.

questions.objective_ref: Use else and finally clauses to separate the success path from guaranteed cleanup (finally always runs).
questions.feedback: finally runs in every case — success, caught exception, or uncaught — making it the place for guaranteed cleanup.
